perturbation.original_text: The Contractor agrees that if the terms and conditions of this Agreement are violated, the Contractor and its employees and agents may be subject to administrative, civil or cri minal action.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor agrees that if the terms and conditions of this Agreement are violated, the Contractor and its employees and agents may be subject to having to buy donuts for the CAO staff.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the potential consequence of violating the NDA to this section, but dramatically reducing the severity to something trivial, misleads the reader about the legal repercussions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1905, which establishes criminal penalties for the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information by government employees or contractors. By suggesting a trivial consequence (buying donuts), the revised text obscures the potential for significant legal repercussions, including criminal charges.

L. 104–294, title VI, § 601(a)(8) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3498 ; Pub. L. 107–347, title II, § 209(d)(2) , Dec. 17, 2002 , 116 Stat. 2930 ; Pub. L. 110–289, div. A, title I, §
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the legal obligations outlined in 18 U.S. Code § 1905. The original text acknowledges potential 'administrative, civil or criminal action' for violating the agreement, while the changed text replaces these serious repercussions with a trivial consequence: 'having to buy donuts for the CAO staff.' This significantly weakens the deterrent effect of the original clause and misleads the reader about the potential legal consequences of violating the agreement. 18 U.S. Code § 1905, as stated in both scraped snippets, establishes criminal penalties for unauthorized disclosure of confidential information, including fines, imprisonment, and removal from office or employment. The modified text's trivial consequence directly contradicts the severity of penalties established by law.

perturbation.original_text: All documents containing Confidential Information and all copies thereof shall be promptly returned to the CAO upon the CAO’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents containing Confidential Information and all copies thereof shall be promptly returned to the CAO upon the CAO’s request. This clause does not apply if the Contractor deems it more convenient to shred them.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that allows the Contractor to shred documents contradicts the original clause and creates ambiguity regarding the return of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1519, which prohibits the destruction or alteration of records with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence a federal investigation. By allowing the contractor to shred documents, the revised text potentially enables the destruction of evidence relevant to a federal investigation, thereby violating this statute.
perturbation.location: Paragraph regarding return of confidential information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy U.S. Code prev | next Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any department or agency of the United States or any case filed under title 11, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case, shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause that allows the contractor to shred documents, creating a direct contradiction with the requirement to return all confidential information to the CAO upon request. This ability to shred documents could violate 18 U.S. Code § 1519, which criminalizes the destruction or alteration of records with the intent to impede or obstruct a federal investigation, as stated in both scraped law snippets. The modification empowers the contractor to destroy potentially relevant documents, undermining the original obligation and potentially enabling a violation of federal law. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor agrees that hereafter it shall (i) treat all Confidential Information confidentially, and (ii) use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating the possible procurement of Technology Services (and, if such procurement is made, for the purpose of performing such Technology Services).
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor agrees that hereafter it shall (i) treat all Confidential Information confidentially, except on Tuesdays, and (ii) use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating the possible procurement of Technology Services (and, if such procurement is made, for the purpose of performing such Technology Services).
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'except on Tuesdays' creates an exception to the confidentiality agreement, directly contradicting the requirement to treat all information confidentially at all times. This renders the confidentiality clause unenforceable on Tuesdays.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally
perturbation.law_explanation: The 'except on Tuesdays' clause directly contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1905, which prohibits the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information. This exception creates a loophole allowing for disclosure on a specific day, undermining the overall confidentiality requirement and potentially exposing sensitive information in violation of the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception ('except on Tuesdays') to the confidentiality agreement, directly contradicting the requirement to treat all information confidentially. This exception undermines the comprehensive confidentiality requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1905, prohibits the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information. The added exception creates a loophole allowing for disclosure on Tuesdays, which could expose sensitive information, thus resulting in a clear non-compliance with the law outlined in the snippets and making the confidentiality obligation unenforceable on Tuesdays.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor understands that the CAO may seek any remedy available to it to ensure compliance with this Agreement, including, but not limited to, application for a court order prohibiting the disclosure of Confidential Information and/or disqualification from the solicitation and contracting process.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor understands that the CAO may seek any remedy available to it to ensure compliance with this Agreement, including, but not limited to, offering the contractor a strongly worded letter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the remedies to only include a 'strongly worded letter' severely weakens the enforcement mechanism and misrepresents the actual legal recourse available to the CAO.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, limiting the CAO's recourse to a 'strongly worded letter,' directly contradicts the potential for criminal penalties under 18 U.S. Code § 1905. The original text correctly implies the potential for serious legal action, while the revision obscures this possibility, misrepresenting the consequences of violating the agreement and thus conflicting with the implications of the statute.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces potential legal remedies (court orders, disqualification) with a 'strongly worded letter,' which significantly weakens the CAO's ability to enforce the agreement. This contradicts the implications of 18 U.S. Code § 1905, as the original text implies serious legal consequences for disclosing confidential information, while the changed text obscures this possibility. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that violators of 18 U.S. Code § 1905 'shall be fined under this title, or imprisoned not more than one year, or both; and shall be removed from office or employment,' which are far more serious than a 'strongly worded letter.' The modification removes the original text's implied alignment with the severe penalties described in the law, creating a legal contradiction.
